data.title: Why S&P Global's 'Lost Years' Could Set Up The Next Decade
data.description:
It has been a long time since I published my last article about S&P Global, Inc. (SPGI) – 4.5 years to be precise. Back then, I argued that the merger made sense, but I saw S&P Global far from being cheap and rated the stock as a “Hold”.

When looking back at the performance during this timeframe, the rating certainly was right. S&P Global is trading for almost the same stock price as back then (it declined 2%) and this is certainly not a good return over such a long timeframe. Especially when comparing S&P Global to the performance of the S&P 500, which increased 50% in the same timeframe, the broader market would have been a much better investment.

With a stock stagnating for several years and the business continuing to grow, we could assume that S&P Global is a better investment today and the company grew into its high valuation multiple. In the following article, I will show that S&P Global is more reasonably valued than it was 4.5 years ago. However, we should not ignore the risks posed by GenAI and I will analyze what impact this might have on the business. Despite acknowledging these risks, I see S&P Global being protected by its wide economic moat and the risk for its different business segments seems rather limited, making it a cautious “Buy” at this point.

Valuation Multiples

We start by looking at the valuation multiples of S&P Global. In my last article, the high valuation multiples and the stock trading above its intrinsic value were one of the biggest problems and the main reason not to invest. Back then, S&P Global was trading for 40 times earnings and now it is trading for 28.5 times earnings. Additionally, the stock is trading for 23.6 times free cash flow.

Looking at the last ten years (or to be more precise: The timeframe since April 1, 2016 as I included the data before due to the P/FCF spike that would have made the chart difficult to read), we see S&P Global trading below the 10-year average for the P/E ratio (which was 33.63) and for the P/FCF ratio (which was 29.16). And at least in the last few years, the stock is trading for one of the lowest valuation multiples.

In the last few quarters, valuation multiples declined from almost 60 times earnings and 50 times free cash flow but only based on these simple valuation multiples we can’t argue that S&P Global is a bargain at this point.

Last Results

On February 10, 2026, the company reported fourth quarter and full year results for fiscal 2025. Revenue increased from $14,208 million in fiscal 2024 to $15,336 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 7.95% year-over-year growth. Operating income increased 16.1% year-over-year from $5,580 million in the previous year to $6,478 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted net income increased from $12.35 in fiscal 2024 to $14.66 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 18.7% year-over-year bottom line growth.

Looking at the full-year results, it seems like growth rates are even improving. Revenue increased from $3,592 million in Q4/24 to $3,916 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 9.0% year-over-year top line growth. Operating profit increased 27.9% year-over-year from $1,309 million in the same quarter last year to $1,674 million in this quarter. Diluted net income per share increased from $2.85 in Q4/24 to $3.75 in Q4/25 – resulting in 31.6% year-over-year bottom line growth.

We can also make the argument that revenue growth is broad-based as all five different business segments are contributing to revenue growth.

Protected From GenAI?

In the last few months, several companies from the software-infrastructure, software applications, financial data, as well as asset management declined steeply and this sector clearly underperformed the S&P 500.

In this steep sell-off, several competitors of S&P Global were hit hard. Recently I published an article about Morningstar, Inc. (MORN), which declined 50% from its previous all-time high. Another company is FactSet Research Systems (FDS), which declined 58% from its previous all-time high.

However, it is also noteworthy that S&P Global and Moody’s Corporation (MCO) have declined from the previous all-time highs, but the declines were much more moderate compared to companies like Morningstar or FactSet Research. We can make the argument that Morningstar’s business is different from S&P Global, and FactSet Research is also generating its revenue from different sources. However, S&P Global is also generating its revenue from ratings, from providing market intelligence as well as indices – very similar to the other companies.

Therefore, we take a similar approach as in my Morningstar article and analyze S&P Global and whether the company is in trouble due to GenAI. We start by looking at company-wide metrics and then focus on the five different segments individually.

Company

One sign that a business is in trouble is growth rates that are slowing down. When looking at S&P Global, we see quarterly growth rates fluctuating heavily over time, but I don’t really see growth rates slowing down in the last few years (fluctuations are heavily tied to cyclical businesses like the rating business – we will get to this).

S&P Global is also optimistic for growth rates to remain high. Organic revenue is expected to grow between 6% and 8% (in constant currencies) and diluted earnings per share are expected to be between $19.40 and $19.65. When comparing this amount to adjusted earnings per share of $17.83, we get a growth rate of 9% to 10% for the bottom line. Additionally, S&P Global is expecting its profit margin to improve by 10 to 35 bps and capital expenditures will be between $215 million and $225 million.

Additionally, we can look at the company’s margins and return on invested capital (and return on equity). In the chart, we can clearly see how the acquisition of IHS Markit and the steep decline in margins as well as return on invested capital. On the other hand, we see margins and return on invested capital improving again in the years since 2022 and we should not draw the conclusion that S&P Global is under pressure due to the threat of GenAI at this point.

Ratings

Now, let’s look at the different business segments individually and we start with one of the company’s core business segments – ratings. In the above mentioned article about Morningstar I wrote that credit ratings are protected quite well from new companies entering this market. And while Morningstar is holding only a small market share, S&P Global has a market share in the credit rating business around 30% - together with Moody's Corporation and Fitch Ratings those three companies dominate this market.

We also have to keep in mind that credit ratings are built on trust and the brand name of S&P (or Moody's) is extremely important. A new company might be able to offer cheaper ratings, but it will probably have to prove over a long time it can provide high quality and reliable ratings before another company can establish itself as a serious credit rating business. This is generating a wide economic moat around this business that is difficult to overcome and lowering costs by using GenAI certainly won't be enough for new competitors.

Indices

In theory, it is easy for new competitors to offer indices of their own and GenAI could certainly be useful for companies to make this process more efficient and maybe also cheaper. However, when also focusing on costs and prices, we are missing the point. Similar to ratings, these indices are based on brand names and the fact they are established in the financial world. Indices like the Down Jones or S&P 500 are legitimized in the financial world and it will be difficult for other companies to establish indices that are used and accepted. This is generating a wide economic moat around the business segment and will help S&P Global to fight off the GenAI threat.

Mobility Spin-Off

While I see limited threats to the ratings business and the indices business, it is getting a little more difficult now. The three remaining segments (Market Intelligence, Energy and Mobility) can be seen as similar and we start with the Mobility segment.

In April 2025, S&P Global announced its intent to spin-off the mobility segment into a standalone public company. Management is aiming to simplify the business and will focus on its remaining four business segments. The spin-off will probably be completed in late 2026 or early 2027 and although the mobility segment will no longer be a part of S&P Global, it is still important for shareholders, who will receive shares in the new business. And at least in the recent past it was one of the business segments reporting slightly higher growth rates, but the remaining four business segments will also grow in the high-single or even double digits (we will get to this).

Market Intelligence and Energy

In the following section, we look at the market intelligence and energy segment and S&P Global is generating its revenue mostly from subscriptions here. And while this subscription business was seen as very sticky and predictable in the last decade, this might change in the years to come. Several articles (including one from the Financial Times and the IDC) are indicating that seat-based subscription models might be replaced by new models. IDC is writing:

Such a shift has profound implications for how SaaS is bought and sold. The traditional per-user, per-month licensing model becomes increasingly obsolete as digital labor replaces manual interaction. IDC predicts that by 2028, pure seat-based pricing will be obsolete, with 70% of software vendors refactoring their pricing strategies around new value metrics, such as consumption, outcomes, or organizational capability.

And such a shift will have an impact on consistency of revenue growth, but it does not necessarily lead to lower growth rates or declining revenue. Aside from a potential shift of the payment and billing methods, the question remains unanswered whether new competitors that might use GenAI can attack the market intelligence business and erode it over time.

And I would follow the argument, management made during the last Investor Day when they addressed the question whether the company is being affected by GenAI. Aside from strong IP protection, which should not be underestimated, it is especially the proprietary data that is key here. Without much doubt, GenAI can generate reports, analyze data or draw conclusions from data in a very effective (and maybe cheaper) way. Nevertheless, the raw data is necessary, and companies still must pay for the data, and customers have to turn to companies like S&P Global to get this data.

And especially for highly specialized data (including Mobility and Energy) I see even higher switching costs as it is often more difficult to acquire this kind of data (compared to stock or bond performance data, which can also be accessed on other platforms).

I don’t know if I actually see 95% of revenue protected, but I would also be optimistic that competition for S&P Global is rather coming from Moody’s or FactSet Research and not some new GenAI based applications. S&P Global can also include AI applications in its services and therefore reduce the incentives to switch to a competitor.

Growth Rates

To sum up, analysts are quite optimistic that strong growth will continue in the years to come and the risk stemming from competitors or GenAI is rather limited. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2030, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.4% over these five years.

These are very similar expectations to management’s mid-term guidance offered during the last Investor Day (November 2025). During the next 3 to 5 years, S&P Global is expecting revenue to grow between 7% and 9% on an organic basis (and in constant currency) with S&P Dow Jones Indices growing even 10% to 12%. Combined with margin expansion (50 to 75 bps per year) management is expecting adjusted diluted earnings per share to grow in the double digits. Note: S&P Global Mobililty is not included in the guidance anymore due to the spin-off (see section above).

This is also in line with past growth rates. In the last ten years, revenue increased with a CAGR of 11.18% and earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 13.29%.

S&P Global is also starting to use share buybacks in a more aggressive way in the last few years. Since the beginning of 2022 (and following the all-stock merger between S&P Global and HIS Market in 2021) the company bought back 53 million shares worth $21 billion. And during the last Investor Day, management announced another huge share buyback program and made clear that buybacks will remain a major capital allocation tool for the company:

And we plan to maintain our framework of returning about 85% of adjusted free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks in a typical year.

The majority of that capital return will be through buybacks as you've experienced. We plan to continue our status as a dividend monarch and raise our dividend each year as we've done for the last 50 years. And to implement this plan, we're excited to announce, as you saw this morning that the Board has authorized a repurchase of another 30 million shares, which we'll start to execute on sometime in the beginning of next year.

S&P Global is also trying to grow by acquisitions. Recently, the company close the acquisition of Enertel AI for an undisclosed amount and integrated Drift AI, an AI-powered Excel solution. Both acquisitions won't have a measurable impact on the company's bottom line, but it is showing how S&P Global can (and must likely will) address the GenAI challenge: By including such applications into its platform.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a last step, let’s calculate an intrinsic value for S&P Global by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a discount rate of 10% and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (302.1 million). As a basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters (which was $5,456 million) and in my opinion, this seems like a reasonable assumption. The free cash flow margin in the last four quarters was 35.6% and therefore only a little bit higher than the 10-year average of 33.7%.

Additionally, we are calculating with 11% growth for the next ten years. This growth rate is not only in line with EPS growth in the last ten years (reported a CAGR of 13.3%) but is also matching management's medium-term financial targets as well as analysts' estimates in the years to come (see section above). While management is providing only targets for the next five years, we can be quite optimistic these growth rates will prevail for the next 10 years due to the wide economic moat. Following these ten years, we calculate with a terminal growth rate of 4% (following the recommendation of CFI). This leads to an intrinsic value of $479.80 and the stock is therefore trading slightly below its intrinsic value.

Conclusion

In my article about Morningstar, I wrote that we get a stock at a wonderful price, but maybe only a fair business. In case of S&P Global, it seems to be reversed: While we are dealing with a wonderful business, the price can be described only as fair.

I personally would like to wait for lower stock prices and hope to get S&P Global for a more attractive price – knowing very well that one of my weaknesses is hoping for too low prices and therefore sometimes missing out on great businesses.

data.title: Assembly Biosciences: 'Buy' On HPI Program Opt-In And HDV Candidate Development
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Assembly Biosciences (ASMB) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Assembly Biosciences: Positive Data Of ABI-5366 Brings 2 Fall Program Catalysts." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was working on the development of two long-acting helicase-primase inhibitors [HPIs], ABI-5366 and ABI-1179, for the treatment of patients with recurrent genital herpes with Gilead Sciences (GILD). The company reported positive data throughout 2025 with respect to the HPI program, which was a huge win for the expected catalysts that took place.

The latest update is that Gilead did end up exercising its option to license both ABI-5366 and ABI-1179. Assembly received a $35 million payment because of it and could also be eligible to earn up to $330 million in milestone payments plus tiered royalties on net sales. In lieu of milestones, the company holds the option to instead do a 40% share of costs and profits. It hasn't chosen this route yet, but it expects to decide this in mid-2026. This is one milestone to consider with respect to this biotech.

With that being said, a phase 2 initiation for this HPI program is expected to happen by the end of 2026. I had a Buy rating the last time around, and I'm going to stick with this rating again. Not just because Gilead exercised its option to license the entire HPI program, but also because it is making a solid move on another front with respect to its pipeline. This would be the development of ABI-6250 for the treatment of patients with Hepatitis Delta Virus [HDV] in a phase 1a study. Speaking of which, positive interim data from this trial was released already, which I will be going over below in detail. There are two catalysts at play with respect to this specific program advancement. The first of which is that Assembly Biosciences is on track to initiate a phase 2 study using ABI-6250 to treat these patients by the end of 2026.

The second of which involves the release of data from the phase 1a study of ABI-6250 for the treatment of these HDV patients as a poster presentation at the upcoming European Association for the Study of the Liver [EASL] Congress taking place in Barcelona, Spain, from May 27-30, 2026. Speaking of presentations, there is another milestone to consider as well, which is that multiple abstracts have been accepted for ABI-5366 and ABI-1179 for presentation at the Congress of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases [ESCMID] taking place in Munich, Germany, from April 17-21, 2026. With Gilead Sciences choosing to license the HPI program [ABI-5366 & ABI-1179], plus the continued development of HDV candidate ABI-6250 in a phase 2 study, I believe that I should maintain a Buy rating on this stock.

Potential To Change The HDV Treatment Landscape With Improved Small Molecule Entry Inhibitor ABI-6250

As I noted above, Assembly Biosciences received good news in that Gilead Sciences chose to exercise its option to license the HPI program consisting of ABI-5366 and ABI-1179. This program is coming along nicely, and phase 2 initiation for it is expected by the end of 2026. Besides the trial initiation expected then, the only other milestone that remains for this program is in mid-2026, when Assembly will decide if it wants to take the 40% share of costs and profits.

For the purpose of this article, I want to go over its development of another candidate being evaluated in the pipeline. This would be the oral small molecule entry inhibitor ABI-6250 for the treatment of patients with chronic Hepatitis Delta Virus. Before going over the release of the phase 1a interim data from this program, plus any catalysts to be released from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Hepatitis Delta Virus [HDV] is characterized as a liver infection as a result of the HDV. What makes this devastating is that it is the most severe form of viral hepatitis known to humans. There is another item to consider, which makes it really bad and hard to treat. For a person to get HDV, they need to already have the Hepatitis B Virus [HBV]. Why is that? That's because the HDV needs HBV in order to reproduce. It is estimated that a total of 290 million people worldwide are infected with HBV, and out of them, between 15 and 20 million are also affected with HDV.

Why is this a bad thing? It is bad enough to have HBV, but having coinfection with HDV makes matters that much worse. The reason why is because it speeds up the progress of liver disease. Patients co-infected with both can see a rapid increase of:

  • Liver cancer
  • Liver failure
  • Cirrhosis
  • Liver fibrosis.

As you can see, having a co-infection of both HBV and HDV makes matters worse. Consider that about 70% of patients with HDV progress to cirrhosis within 10 years [Assembly Biosciences Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #30].

This is all good, but how exactly does ABI-6250 actually work in treating these HDV patients? It works by acting as an HDV entry inhibitor. It goes a bit deeper than this. The thing is that liver cells host a transport molecule known as Sodium Taurocholate Co-transporting Polypeptide [NTCP]. This candidate is responsible for blocking the binding and entry of HBV and HDV into the liver cell membrane. The endgame goal of preventing this is to reduce infection of liver cells. With that being said, ABI-6250 The NTCP is also responsible for the transport of bile acids as well.

There is a drug known as BULEVIRTIDE, which is a large molecule entry inhibitor. The point of this drug is that it has a similar mechanism of action [MOA] of blocking the NTCP transport channel of liver cells. This drug acts as a "plug the hole" mechanism of NTCP, which blocks viral entry of HBV and HDV. It is the only approved HDV drug in Europe, Australia, and Canada only. The downside is that patients have to receive BULEVIRTIDE daily as an injection. Not only that, but it requires cold storage as well, as another disadvantage. This is where a drug from Assembly Biosciences like ABI-6250 might come into play. It is an oral small molecule entry inhibitor. The bottom line is that entry inhibition for HBV and HDV is a validated target shown in BULEVIRTIDE. Where both viral reductions and ALT normalization have been observed [Assembly Biosciences Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #31].

The hope is that Assembly Biosciences can show its drug to achieve the same MOA of blocking entry of HDV in a significant fashion to help these HDV patients. This is a large market opportunity as well. The global Hepatitis D market size is expected to reach $1.09 billion by 2035. The thing is that the company has the ability to accomplish two things here with ABI-6250. The first of which is that there are no FDA-approved drugs for HDV. Secondly, it could potentially be able to offer the first oral HDV therapy. To accomplish its ability to test out whether or not this entry inhibitor would work, it launched a phase 1a study known as ABI-6250-101. This trial was split into testing both a single-ascending dose [SAD] and a multiple-ascending dose [MAD] design.

It was noted first and foremost that bile acid synthesis was increased. This is a good thing in the sense that bile acid is observed to increase when NTCP is blocked [Assembly Biosciences Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #34]. In terms of safety, the drug was good for the most part. There was one Grade 2 ALT elevation in the liver with the 25 mg dose of ABI-6250. However, all the other dose cohorts had observed ALT elevations at low frequency.

Not only that, but the company was able to move on with a dose of 1 mg of ABI-6250 or lower in the MAD portion of this phase 1a study. The most important item was the mean half-life of 4 days observed for it. This is important because this half-life supports the daily oral dosing that Assembly plans to move on with. The key thing for this program is what is going to happen going forward. With that being said, the goal is to initiate a phase 2 study evaluating the use of ABI-6250 for the treatment of patients with HDV by the end of 2026. This is a long time to wait, yes, but there is another catalyst to consider for this program in the meantime. It is said that the company will present a poster of phase 1a data for this program at the European Association for the Study of the Liver [EASL] Congress taking place in Barcelona, Spain, between May 27-30, 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Assembly Biosciences had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $248.1 million as of December 21, 2025. The truth is that the company is in good shape in terms of cash. In its Q4 of 2025 and end of 2025 financial highlights, it mentions that it received a $35 million payment from Gilead for the option fee of the licensing of the HPI program. The company has been able to obtain a lot of revenue from Gilead because of its ongoing collaboration agreements. Consider that the revenue generated from Gilead for the year ending December 31, 2025, was $72.3 million.

Not only that, but its cash projection runway it lays out is highly bullish as well. It notes in the same press release that it believes its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations into 2028. This cuts the risk of dilution in the near term, and the company has plenty of cash to meet several of its expected milestones in 2026 and beyond. The company burns roughly $84.4 million of cash per year. This is broken down into $64.8 million in R&D expenses and then $19.6 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks for investors to be aware of before investing in Assembly Biosciences. The first risk to consider would be with the expected initiation of the phase 2 study using ABI-6250 as an oral viral entry inhibitor for the treatment of patients with HDV. Such a study is expected to kick off at the end of this year.

The risk here is that even though the company was able to show that BULEVIRTIDE as a large molecule entry inhibitor can block the NTCP transport channel effectively, there is no assurance that ABI-6250 will be able to show a similar outcome in the phase 2 study. While the phase 1a interim data was positive in showing that oral daily dosing is possible [thanks to the pharmacokinetic profile], the study still remained blinded with the placebo. There is no assurance that the drug will ultimately be able to do better than placebo in blocking the viral entry load of HDV in a phase 2 study.

The second risk to consider would be with Gilead Sciences opting in to license the HPI program with both clinical candidates of ABI-5366 and ABI-1179. The goal is for Gilead to initiate a phase 2 study using these candidates to treat patients with recurrent genital herpes by the end of this year.

The risk here is that even though an interim analysis of a phase 1b study showed that once-weekly 350 mg of ABI-5366 over a 29-day period was able to achieve a 94% reduction of HSV-2 shedding rate compared to placebo [p<0.01], there is no guarantee that this will be replicated again in the expected phase 2 study. Another risk to consider then would be that of this collaboration agreement. If weaker than expected results are generated from this mid-stage trial, then Gilead could potentially choose to terminate the agreement of this HPI program. That would either put milestone payments/tiered royalties at risk or, if chosen, the 40% cost-share profit structure.

The company does have another candidate in its pipeline that suffered a major blow. This is with respect to the development of capsid assembly modulator [CAM] ABI-4334 for the treatment of patients with the Hepatitis B virus [HBV]. The end result is that Gilead Sciences chose not to exercise its option to license ABI-4334 for the treatment of these patients. The company obtained sole rights again for this clinical candidate, and the hope is that it is going to be able to find a partner for this program.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to find a partner willing to fund this candidate into human clinical testing. If it doesn't find a partner to advance this candidate forward, then it is unlikely that it will spend funds to develop this on its own. It is not guaranteed but possible that it could put an end to this program or decide not to continue to develop it.

Conclusion

Assembly Biosciences has done well to obtain positive interim results from its phase 1b study using ABI-5366 to treat patients with recurrent genital herpes. As I have shown above, it was able to obtain a 94% reduction of the HSV-2 shedding rate from a phase 1b study. With the positive data generated from this interim analysis, Gilead ended up deciding to exercise its option to license the HPI program consisting of both ABI-5366 and ABI-1179 for the treatment of patients with recurrent genital herpes.

As a matter of fact, this program is pressing forward, with Gilead expected to initiate a phase 2 study for it by the end of 2026. The beauty of this biotech is that it is making headway to take what BULEVIRTIDE has been able to achieve in blocking NTCP and be an effective HDV viral entry inhibitor and develop its own candidate. It is not just copying what has been shown already. As I have shown above, it is generating a few particular enhancements. First and foremost, it has developed ABI-6250 as a small molecule inhibitor that can be given to patients via the oral administration route.

In essence, not requiring it to be given via injection and not requiring cold storage either. Most notably, to attempt to give U.S. patients with HDV a treatment option. BULEVIRTIDE works well for long-term use, but beyond being plagued with these disadvantages, it is not approved for the United States. With Gilead exercising its opt-in option to license the HPI program consisting of ABI-5366 and ABI-1179 for the targeting of patients with recurrent genital herpes, plus the expected initiation of the phase 2 study using ABI-6250 for the treatment of patients with HDV by the end of 2026, I believe that I should maintain a Buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Goodbye Bull Market, Hello Recession
data.description:
Many of us agree that the U.S. economy is slowing down, but there are a couple of views on where it goes from here. One view suggests this is just a mid-cycle slowdown that will resolve to the upside when some growth catalysts kick in. However, the other view is that the slowdown will worsen and eventually lead to a recession. The National Bureau of Economic Research, or "NBER" is the private, non-profit organization that is the official arbiter when it comes to declaring recessions. The last time the NBER declared a recession was in 2020, when the pandemic caused what was the shortest recession on record, as it officially lasted only two months.

One rule of thumb for the definition of a recession is often cited as being two consecutive quarters of declining GDP, but the NBER uses a broader definition, which is "a significant decline in economic activity spread across the economy, lasting more than a few months." However, the 2020 pandemic was so deep and widespread that the NBER immediately classified it as a recession, even though the economy started to rebound rapidly due to the massive amount of government stimulus. This 2020 recession was so brief that it was almost over before it started, making it just a blip for many of us. That means we must look back to the 2007 to 2009 Great Financial Crisis, or "GFC", if we want to consider the last recession that had some duration.

The GFC recession was nearly 20 years ago, which means that there is a whole generation of people and investors who have never experienced a serious and long-lasting recession. I think there is a lot of complacency when it comes to considering the risks of a recession since so many investors have not experienced a serious one. Many also seem to believe that the Federal Reserve can ward off a recession with some rate cuts and stimulus.

I think of recessions as a financial storm that clears out the excesses and ends weak business models, just as a weather-related storm takes out the weak limbs of trees and destroys whatever wasn't built to last. Similar to a weather-related storm, the longer we go without a recession (or financial storm), the worse it could be, because so many excesses have built up for many years. Some of these potential excesses seem to be showing up in the private credit market. I worry that as nearly 20 years have passed since our last serious recession, the next recession could be more severe. Just as recession concerns mount, multiple signs suggest the bull market has lost momentum and may now be over. With this in mind, let's examine several indicators that signal a recession is very likely, which could also indicate the bull market is over:

The Bull Market Might Be Over

For the past few months, the stock market hasn't broken out to new highs, which could signal that the market has reached a top. Some market-leading stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) have reported very strong results and order backlogs, yet that stock has been treading water for weeks and remains well below the highs it hit in late October 2025. When stocks fail to rise even after great news is announced, it often signals that they have peaked and that a downturn looms. As shown in the chart below, the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has also been treading water for the past few months and has recently declined down to around the 200-day moving average. This gives me some hope that the market could stabilize here since this should be a level of strong support.

However, the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) chart shows a very bearish double top formation since it hit the highs in October and then again in January. Most recently, it has declined to around the 200-day moving average. With both the SPY and QQQ now trading very close to their respective 200-day moving averages, the market needs to stabilize here and rebound soon if the bulls want to stay in charge. I do think a rebound is likely, as I don't expect the war in Iran to last much longer. While I am expecting a rebound from a ceasefire or some de-escalation, I see this as an opportunity to sell into strength. After a potential relief rally if the Iran war de-escalates, I believe the market will get back to focusing on the weakening job market due to AI-related layoffs, consumers showing financial strain, a struggling housing market because of high mortgage rates, and historically high stock market valuations.

#1 Recession Indicator: The Sahm Rule

The Sahm Rule is a highly accurate recession predictor triggered when the three-month moving average of the unemployment rate rises by 0.5% or more above its 12-month low. The Sahm Rule was officially triggered after the jobs report released on March 6, 2026, showed a loss of about 92,000 jobs in February. History shows that every single time the Sahm Rule has been triggered over the last 60 years, the unemployment rate has worsened, and a recession has followed. That's a track record that I don't want to bet against.

#2 Recession Indicator: The Inverted Yield Curve Is Beginning To Un-Invert

As shown in the chart below, the yield curve remained inverted for a record-breaking period throughout 2024 and 2025. The yield curve represents the difference between long-term and short-term interest rates. An inverted yield curve has predicted the past seven recessions, making it an important indicator. It's notable that the yield curve has recently begun to un-invert (or steepen), with the 10-year yield now roughly 0.50% above the 2-year yield. This is a warning sign for the economy because when the yield curve begins to un-invert rapidly, it usually happens just when recessions are getting started, as the financial markets begin to price in emergency rate cuts from the Federal Reserve.

#3 Recession Indicator: GDP Growth Is Decelerating

According to the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, or "BEA", real GDP grew by only 0.7% in the fourth quarter of 2025. This marks a sharp slowdown from the 4.4% growth in the third quarter of 2025. This decline resulted from a sharp drop in government spending and weaker consumer consumption. With oil prices rising rapidly in recent weeks, this could put even more pressure on GDP growth in the coming quarters. With GDP growth of less than 1% in Q4 of 2025, it is dangerously close to growth going negative, and that would be a classic sign of a recession.

#4 Recession Indicator: Economically Sensitive Stocks Have Been Plunging

If you look at many economically sensitive stocks, this raises another concern: the stock market may be starting to price in growing recession risks. Look at the charts below of stocks like Goodyear Tire (GT), Capital One Financial (COF), or Whirlpool (WHR), and you will see how poorly stocks in three very different but economically sensitive sectors are trading. I view this as another sign of a potentially looming recession.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I expect oil prices to decline significantly in the coming months, either due to demand destruction or, most likely, because some type of de-escalation occurs with the Iran war. I believe this will give the stock market a solid rebound, and I plan to sell more positions into this strength. However, I could be wrong, because if oil prices decline significantly, it could be enough to help get GDP growth back on track. Lower oil prices could also ease the concerns about rising inflation from the Fed, and that could lead to more rate cuts in 2026.

Also, larger tax refunds are starting to be received by many consumers, and this could lead to increased spending in the coming quarters and create better-than-expected GDP growth. The stock market has been very resilient, and it could be at a support level right now. It could surprise to the upside, and investor sentiment could improve if some high-profile IPOs are successful later this year. In addition, this is a midterm election year, and because of this, I expect President Trump will possibly introduce policies that could boost the economy in the next few months, and this could also boost stocks.

In Summary

Many stocks are oversold now, and the market is near key technical support levels. As such, I don't believe this is the time to sell, which is why I have a hold rating on the market overall. I believe the Iran war will de-escalate soon, which could spark a major relief rally when oil prices potentially plunge. I plan to use this rally to sell into strength and raise even more cash for the recession that I believe is coming. While there are pockets of value in the stock market now, valuations are at historically rich levels, and a recession could reset values to much lower levels. I have been raising cash for months now, and I expect to deploy it when stocks are potentially trading at much more attractive valuations.

data.title: Why Eli Lilly Remains My Top Obesity Bet
data.description:
At the end of October, I published an article on Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), "Eli Lilly: Is Another Monster Quarter Coming."

In just over four months, Eli Lilly shares rose 11.7%, outperforming the 'barometer' for the biotech investors, the iShares Biotechnology [3.5% return] ETF (IBB), as well as the S&P 500 [-4.3% return] (SPY).

In my view, the ongoing bullish momentum will continue despite HSBC's downgrade of Eli Lilly on March 17, thanks to the strong sales of its GLP-1 drugs, as well as the expected FDA decision on orforglipron on April 10th, which will be a direct rival to Novo Nordisk's Wegovy (NVO).

Also, on March 19th, Eli Lilly finally announced the results of its Phase 3 TRANSCEND-T2D-1 study [NCT06354660].

It evaluated retatrutide [4 mg/9 mg/12 mg], a triple agonist of the GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors.

For better understanding, I'll remind you that Novo Nordisk's Wegovy/Ozempic is only a GLP-1 agonist, while LLY's flagship, Mounjaro, activates both the GIP and GLP-1 receptors.

In the trial, a group of people with type 2 diabetes without obesity taking 12 mg retatrutide achieved average A1C reductions by 1.9%, compared to 0.8% in those taking a placebo in 40 weeks of treatment.

Also, I was glad to see that Eli Lilly's synthetic peptide is now the number one in weight reduction on the market.

So, taking it resulted in a weight loss of 15.1 kg vs. to only 2.7 kg in the placebo arm.

By comparison, the 15 mg tirzepatide [Mounjaro] group showed a 7.8 kg reduction in body weight in the Phase 3 SURPASS-1 study [NCT03954834].

But still, I wonder. Is according to my "ALLKA 4D rating" model Eli Lilly's risk/reward profile still attractive?

Demand for Kisunla has accelerated

As you know, Kisunla [donanemab] is a monoclonal antibody targeting β-amyloid, first approved by the FDA on July 2, 2024, that is, about a year and a half later than Biogen/Eisai's Leqembi [lecanemab] (BIIB) (ESALF).

In my opinion, the key advantage of Leqembi is its safety profile.

So, in the Phase 3 Clarity AD study [NCT03887455], about 13% of patients in the lecanemab group experienced sulcal effusions/cerebral edema, known as ARIA-E.

By comparison, in the TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 study [NCT04437511], 24% people with Alzheimer's disease who took donanemab experienced this adverse event.

However, I believe that, as during the COVID-19 pandemic, neurologists have been able to adapt how to deal with ARIA-E and ARIA-hemosiderin cases.

As a result, which of these anti-amyloid mAbs is more effective in slowing cognitive/functional decline will become a primary consideration for the HCPs.

So, in NCT03887455, the use of Leqembi led to a reduction by 1.21 points in the CDR-SB from baseline at 18 months, compared to 1.66 in the placebo arm.

Well, to put it simply, treatment with it slowed the decline in this scale by 0.45, or 27% [P=0.00005].

Meanwhile, Kisunla slowed deterioration on CDR-SB by 29% or 0.7 [P<0.0001] at week 76.

Thus, my first intermediate conclusion is that in an indirect comparison, Kisunla was shown to be superior to Leqembi.

By the way, I see a strong correlation between this data and the sales of this "duo."

Sales of donanemab were up $39 million quarter-over-quarter to $109 million in Q4, while lecanemab sales increased by just $12 million.

Zepbound and Mounjaro are actively capturing the obesity-diabetes drugs market

Both of these medications consist of the same active substance called tirzepatide.

And although sales of Mounjaro exceed those of the higher-dose formulation of the dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist, Zepbound, I believe that it will become the flagship in Eli Lilly’s portfolio in the long term, in part due to the faster rate of its label expansions.

So, in Q4, Zepbound generated $4.26 billion in net revenue for LLY, up 123.4% year-on-year and by 18.8% QoQ.

It also beat my "best case" scenario by $112 million.

In my opinion, this is due to Eli Lilly's switching patients from Trulicity, whose exclusivity in the U.S. expires in 2027, as well as stronger demand for Zepbound from people living with OSA [obstructive sleep apnea].

Overall, CFO Lucas Montarce was also quite encouraged by the progress in developing the tirzepatide franchise.

U.S. Zepbound revenue more than doubled compared to Q4 2024. Zepbound continues to be the market leader in the branded obesity market with nearly 70% share of new prescriptions. U.S. total prescription growth for Zepbound was robust, both in auto-injectors and vials.

We are encouraged to see sustained growth uptake of Zepbound vials, which represented approximately 1/3 of total Zepbound prescriptions and nearly 50% of new Zepbound prescriptions in Q4.

In the U.S., Mounjaro expanded market leadership in the type 2 diabetes incretin market, exiting the quarter with over 55% of new prescriptions.

And one of the latest milestones I must mention is the FDA's approval on February 23rd of the four-dose single-patient use KwikPen, which provides a month's supply of Zepbound in one device.

Also, on January 8th, Eli Lilly published promising results from the Phase 3b TOGETHER-PsA study [NCT06588296].

In it, 33.5% of patients with an autoimmune condition called psoriatic arthritis taking the combination of Taltz [ixekizumab] and Zepbound achieved ACR50, compared to only 20.4% of those in the Taltz arm.

In other words, adding tirzepatide, as you know, the most effective medication for the treatment of obesity, also leads to a significant improvement in psoriatic disease activity.

This is another prerequisite for the broader use of dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor co-agonists, not just for the weight management or the treatment of sleep apnea and type 2 diabetes.

Risks

When it comes to Eli Lilly, it's quite difficult for me to find the risks that could adversely impact its FCF, given that Zepbound and Mounjaro, as I said earlier, are the most effective drugs on the GLP-1/GIP bi-agonist market.

However, one of the "dark spots" in its portfolio is Trulicity, whose U.S. patents expire in 2027, according to LLY's 10-K. That is, I expect the launch of its biosimilars between 2027 and 2029.

By the way, as I noted in the table below, its sales were about $4.28 billion in 2025, or 6.6% of Eli Lilly's total revenue.

Takeaway

I believe that, despite profit-taking pressure following recent good gains, Eli Lilly's risk/reward profile has only become more attractive.

Its revenue rose 42.6% year-over-year to $19.29 billion, beating Wall Street analysts' expectations by $1.35 billion.

And more importantly, Eli Lilly's non-GAAP EPS also came in well above the consensus estimate, as in the past 3 quarters, but this time by 63 cents, thanks to the previously mentioned medications, as well as Verzenio and Omvoh.

In turn, Omvoh, which is an IL-23p19 inhibitor, is, in my view, becoming one of the preferred choices among gastroenterologists for the treatment of two types of idiopathic inflammatory conditions called ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Even With Hormuz Reopened, I Still See The S&P 500 At 6,100
data.description:
I see the market tape deteriorating by the day, as VLCC transits through the Strait of Hormuz still remain at a near standstill. What makes me sweat is the fact that the selloff is broad-based and not limited to large-cap growth stocks. In fact, the Russell 2000 is down 7.3% in the past month vs. the 3.1% drop of the Nasdaq 100.

As I'm typing, Brent oil is above $100, with the spotlight right now on gas prices after this week's Israeli strikes on Iran’s South Pars gas field and Iranian missile strikes on Qatari LNG facilities. For reference, the front-month Dutch TTF gas futures (i.e., the main European gas benchmark) jumped by as much as 35% on March 19 after these headlines.

Call me crazy, but trading the headlines makes little to no sense. That’s why I’m not chasing the highs in the only pocket of the market that is in the green: energy.

My outlook on the broader markets hasn't really changed. I still think the S&P 500 could see the 6,100-6,200 level in the first half of the year after a quick "relief rally" once the war comes to an end.

The catalyst?

Inflation is likely to remain high between March and June (at least), as the oil flows will take a few months to normalize after the conflict eventually comes to an end in the Middle East. Therefore, the post-war relief rally shouldn't last more than a few weeks.

The key idea I discuss in this piece is that inflation will likely remain at the forefront of investors’ minds in the first half of the year. Therefore, I prefer to sit on my hands and keep waiting before I buy any dip.

The March 18 FOMC Meeting

On an 11-1 vote, the FOMC left the target range for the federal funds rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75%.

By far, the most important part of the meeting was the release of the Summary of Economic Projections, given that the market is trying to gauge the secondary effects of the conflict in the Middle East.

I include the table below:

On the GDP front, growth has been revised upward from the December 2025 baseline. Specifically, the median GDP growth for 2026 is now expected at 2.4% vs. 2.3% in the December 2025 estimate. For 2027, the table above shows 2.3% vs. the prior expectation of 2.0%, and the longer-run growth was revised to 2.0% from the prior 1.8%.

Am I surprised? Not really.

As the tech rotation started in October, I've been saying that this risk-off move (see the chart below showing market-cap-weighted returns for the tech sector across different timeframes) has very little to do with fundamentals.

In fact, the Street is more optimistic on EPS growth today than at the end of December 2025.

So, why have growth stocks sold off then?

Simply because investors had to take profits after a 40%+ run between the lows in April and the highs in January. In fact, as one can clearly see on the chart below, the price action in the S&P 500 has been quite ahead of earnings growth after the rally post Liberation Day.

Back to the March SEP release, the median unemployment rate projected for 2026 was 4.4%, unchanged from the December projections. That's bad for stocks, as investors wanted to see more downward pressure on employment to justify more rate cuts in the second half of the year.

On inflation, the median of the headline PCE for 2026 was revised upward to 2.7% vs. 2.4% in December. Interestingly, 2028 remained unchanged, and 2027 was up by just 10 bps vs. the earlier estimate.

In other words, the inflation shock due to the spike in oil prices is likely to be transitory (as long as vessels resume their transits through the Strait of Hormuz in the next few weeks) and short-lived.

I found some of Fed's Waller remarks earlier in March particularly interesting:

You're going to see a spike in gasoline prices. That's what American citizens are going ​to see when they go to the pump, and they're going to ​stare and be a little shocked

If it's ⁠unwound in ... a couple of weeks or even two months, it's not going ​to be a big factor down the road

This is...more like a one-off event

For reference, a (very interesting) Federal Reserve study from 2023 suggests that a 10% increase in oil prices raises energy CPI by about 1.5% on impact, rising to 2.3% after two quarters.

Brent oil prices are up by over 40% since the start of the conflict in Iran. So, multiply by 4x the estimates above.

As a side note, an industry that I've been eyeing this week is recreational vehicles (particularly, RV stocks). As seen in the 1-month return map below, this industry has been hammered since the war began.

There are two forces that pressure cyclicals like the one above. First, higher oil prices deter people from buying large RVs. For reference, a Class A motorhome gets about 6-10 mpg. Outside the RV space, aviation fuel jumped by 50% since the war in Iran started on February 28. The second force is affordability. These are large, often financed, discretionary purchases. Therefore, these industries are very sensitive to interest rates.

Speaking of interest rates, the SEP table shows the median fed-funds midpoint for end-2026 remaining at 3.4%. The expectation for 2027 was unchanged as well (3.1%). The only upward revision was for the longer-run rate, which rose to 3.1% from 3.0%. In terms of the dot plot, I considered including below the latest chart:

For reference, here is the dot plot chart from the December 2025 meeting.

Even though the median is the same, one can clearly see how the distribution for 2026 shifted a bit firmer towards the Hawkish side.

In other words, the Fed expects just one rate cut this year. In fact, the Street's consensus is only pricing in one rate cut in the December 2026 / January 2027 meeting. Other sources (see below) are even more pessimistic and don't project any rate cuts even for the January 2027 meeting.

Why I'm Not Panicking (Yet)

It's time to put my sub-blue-collar, no-nonsense hat on and read the market tape.

First, I still believe we may see a correction in the S&P 500 toward the 6,100 to 6,200 level in the first half of the year. The selloff over the past month goes beyond large-cap growth stocks. In fact, as seen below, small caps have been affected the most, even more than the Mag-7 group.

Why is my target 6,100 to 6,200 for the S&P 500?

Simple. Technical analysis.

For the few readers who didn't click away after my remark above on TA, the trend looks clearly bearish, with the index now below the 200-day MA. The next support seems to be around 6,150.

Considering today’s 6,660 level, that drop represents a 6% move. So, what’s the catalyst?

To start, I don't think the continuation of the war is going to be the catalyst. In fact, I expect the war to end within the next few weeks, which is likely going to lead to a brief relief rally.

I'm planning to ride that brief relief rally with a few short-term positions in pockets of the market that have been pressured by inflation worries. Right now, the industries on my watchlist are:

  • Airlines, especially those with limited hedging against oil prices.
  • Cruises. Same logic, with Carnival currently in my watchlist as it has a high operational exposure in the Mediterranean.
  • Residential construction, due to the fear of higher rates for longer.
  • Mortgage finance. Same reason.
  • Aerospace and defense, as there is a $1.5T catalyst in 2027.
  • High growth tech, with an emphasis on the picks and shovels of the AI data center buildout, namely the computer hardware and semiconductor equipment industries.
  • Pockets in industrials exposed to the AI data center buildout, like the electrical equipment and parts industry.

Aside from aerospace and defense, I'm still debating which industries to buy and hold for the longer term and which to sell for a quick potential profit after the war ends. Because the war will end, right?

First, it appears that the U.S. is not considering sending ground troops to the region. Second, Trump seems to care about the oil and gas infrastructure in the region, after telling Netanyahu not to repeat strikes like the one in Iran’s South Pars gas field. Third, some reports suggest that the Pentagon had asked the White House to approve a request of more than $200 billion for funding the war. This last remark is what makes me believe that the conflict still has a few more weeks. In fact, I think that's precisely why the U.S. is considering unsanctioning 140M barrels of Iranian oil in the next few days. That is enough supply for 10-14 days, according to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. That's my timeline for the end of the conflict.

To be clear, I don’t think the S&P 500 will drop to my price target in 10 to 14 days. So, what catalyst would get it there?

I think the catalyst will be the March CPI. The latest update from the Cleveland Fed nowcast shows headline CPI at 3.02% y/y, core CPI at 2.60% y/y, headline PCE at 3.14% y/y, and core PCE at 2.97% y/y for March:

Looking beyond March, analysts from J.P. Morgan Asset Management estimate that U.S. CPI inflation could now peak at 3.5% in June 2026, and then ease to 1.9% y/y by December 2026 as gasoline prices retreat later in the year.

Interestingly, the peak is projected for June, not March, which makes sense given the fact that it will take a few months for the oil and gas supply to hit the market due to the strikes. In fact, Qatar recently declared force majeure on LNG output after damage to the Ras Laffan export complex. It appears that some officials cited a 3-5 year repair window.

The scenario above is an extreme case. Overall, I think it will take a few months for oil flows around the world to normalize. Therefore, I expect the "relief rally" once the conflict comes to an end to be short-lived. That's why I see the industries above as a quick trade. Overall, I expect inflation to remain the key topic in the first half of the year. That's why I'm still sitting on my hands.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: No Profits, No Bull Case
data.description:
Thesis

Firefly Aerospace Inc. (FLY) is a recently listed space and defense company that mainly works on small rockets for launches and missions to the Moon. The last time I reviewed the stock, I gave it a Hold rating. That’s because, on one hand, the company is winning a good number of contracts (which is positive), but on the other hand, it's spending a lot of cash and it’s still not clear how or when it will become profitable. Today, after reviewing the latest earnings, I’m still not ready to turn bullish given the combination of ongoing losses, heavy spending, and execution risk despite strong growth.

Breaking Down Firefly Aerospace’s Q4 2025 Earnings

  • Q4 Revenue: $57.7M
  • Q3 Revenue: $30.8M
  • Q4 2024 Revenue: $9.0M.

  • FY 2025 Revenue: $159.9M
  • YoY Revenue Growth: +163%
  • Q4 Spacecraft Revenue: $50.0M
  • Q4 Launch Revenue: $7.7M
  • Q4 Gross Margin: 27.7%.

Revenue growth was mainly driven by the spacecraft side of the business. This was the result of steady progress and successfully reaching key milestones on the Blue Ghost landers, Elytra orbiters, and the newly added SciTec programs. The launch segment also benefited from Alpha Flight 7, which is meant to reassure everyone the vital systems are working as Block 2 approaches. Shifting more of the business toward defense and lunar contracts, where revenue's recognized gradually as work is completed (percentage-of-completion accounting) helped stabilize gross margins by ironing out out the inherently lumpy nature of launch events.

  • Q4 GAAP Operating Expenses: $101.6M
  • Q3 GAAP Operating Expenses: $70.7M
  • Q4 2024 GAAP Operating Expenses: $57.1M
  • Q4 Non-GAAP Operating Expenses: $80.5M
  • Q4 GAAP Operating Loss: -$85.6M
  • Q4 Non-GAAP Operating Loss: -$64.5M
  • Q4 GAAP Net Loss: -$41.1M
  • Q4 Non-GAAP Net Loss: -$58.5M
  • Stock-Based Compensation: $12.6M.

You can see that costs went up quite a bit. This was mainly because of the full effect of the SciTec acquisition, plus higher costs from being a public company, and throw in intentional increases in R&D spending, specifically for Alpha upgrades, Eclipse development, and expanding spacecraft manufacturing capacity. The result was bigger operating losses. Although, one-time tax breaks and settlement gains helped cushion the bottom line.

  • Free Cash Flow: -$79.3M
  • Cash & Investments: $893M
  • Revolver Drawn: $260M
  • SciTec Acquisition Cost: $277.4M
  • CapEx Q4: $12.1M.

Free cash flow's still negative. The company spent a lot of money on acquisitions and increased its investments in things like test stands, clean-room expansions, and factory infrastructure. However, the company’s financial position's still strong. It has a solid amount of cash from after its IPO and access to a revolving credit line and gives management room to invest in growth, ideally, without immediately tapping investors or diluting shareholders.

  • Backlog: $1.4B
  • Prior Quarter Backlog: $1.3B
  • YoY Backlog Growth: +22%.

The increasing backlog is the clearest sign of future business. It shows that customers are becoming more involved and committed to the company’s ecosystem. This includes work on national-security payloads, missions for NASA’s lunar program, and new business opportunities in countries like Sweden and Japan.

  • Planned Alpha Launches 2026: 4.

Ultimately, these investments only matter if they reduce risk and improve execution, and recent operational results suggest that’s starting to happen. Management correctly highlights that the reliability team carefully reviewed flight data and improved their processes, which led to a near-perfect Alpha rocket launch and a successful second-stage engine restart. This reduces risk and makes it easier to increase how often they can launch. And that same careful approach can be seen in other areas too, such as the structural testing of Blue Ghost and key separation milestones for Elytra. In addition, SciTec’s FORGE system has proved its value by tracking missiles in real time during recent conflicts.

What makes me a bit less confident is how much work still needs to be done. The company has to ramp up production of Alpha Block 2, while at the same time continuing development of the larger Eclipse vehicle, working on several landers at once, and building AI-enabled defense software.

On the demand side, things look good and track with industry trends. NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman recently made major changes to the Artemis lunar program, calling for more frequent missions to the Moon and the use of standardized spacecraft to keep things from grinding to a halt. This approach actually supports Firefly’s plan to scale its Blue Ghost lunar lander with a modular design.

  • SHIELD Contract Ceiling: $151B (10 years)
  • Golden Dome Opportunity: ~$185B.

At the same time, the Pentagon recently decided to increase funding for the Golden Dome program by an additional $10 billion. That decision points to fully integrated systems as the answer, like the SHIELD-enabled solutions that combine Firefly’s hardware with SciTec’s data-processing technology. Additionally, Alpha’s successful Flight 7 launch on March 11 further strengthens confidence in this approach and its underlying technology.

  • 2026 Revenue Guidance: $420M – $450M
  • Midpoint: $435M
  • Implied YoY Growth: +172%
  • % of 2026 Revenue Already Booked: ~80%.

The midpoint of the company’s guidance suggests very fast growth compared to last year. Of course, that kind of growth depends on everything going according to plan, hitting key milestones on time and maintaining a steady pace of launches. Right now, I’m paying close attention to whether the 80% of business that’s already booked actually stays secure. That could be affected if there are delays in handing over the Eclipse program to Northrop Grumman (NOC), or if government defense spending starts to weaken.

FLY’s Valuation

FLY's still being valued very highly compared to other space companies, even though it's not making a profit yet. Right now, its trailing EV-to-sales ratio is 24.33x, which is extremely high, about 994% higher than the sector median of 2.22x. Looking ahead, its forward EV-to-sales ratio is 5.97x, which is still much higher than the sector median of 2.12x.

Meanwhile, FLY's valuation also looks considerable when you compare it to its sales. Its price-to-sales ratio is 6.93x based on past performance and 8.07x looking forward, which, again, is much higher than the usual benchmarks of 1.85x and 1.76x.

Gross margin's negative 4.91%, meaning the core business is already in the red before the rest of the bills show up. Its EBITDA margin is negative 200.76%, which shows extremely large operating losses, and its levered free cash flow margin is negative 221.63%, tell us it's burning a significant amount of cash.

On top of that, it is spending heavily on investments, with capital expenditures taking up 21.05% of its sales. It’s also increasing its share count by about 1 million shares each quarter while continuing to burn cash. Overall, its future depends on successful Alpha rocket launches, lunar missions, and integrating SciTec, all while facing competition and potential risks from any slowdown or issues in defense spending.

Consensus expects EPS growth to surge from 46% in 2026 to 290% by 2029, with a 2029 P/E near 11.65. At this price, it only works if everything goes perfectly, otherwise the stock gets knocked back to earth.

Final Verdict

All things considered, I'm keeping my Hold rating. Downside risk still leads, with valuation, cash burn, dilution, and execution issues hanging over things despite all the backlog and tailwinds. Growth investors should buy any dips; conservative and value investors should avoid this entirely. The advice I'm giving myself: wait for valuation to compress before entering.

data.title: PDI With PDX: Neutralizing The Premium For A Diversified 11.5% Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When choosing closed-end funds for portfolios, a lot of investors face a common dilemma: buy the flagship PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) at a premium, or look for less popular alternatives that are both cheaper and offer higher total returns. PDI is undoubtedly one of the sector’s benchmarks, for which I maintain my "Buy" rating. Nevertheless, the fund’s total return potential is often limited, with current market volatility amplifying sector-specific risks. Given that there's an effective alternative in the form of its "little brother"—the PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX)—the goal of this article is to present arguments demonstrating the advisability of withdrawing a portion of capital from PDI to combine the two aforementioned instruments in a 50/50 ratio. The investment strategy not only neutralizes the "premium tax" but also increases sector diversification whilst maintaining double-digit dividend yields.

Fundamental Similarities and Differences Between PDI and PDX

Both PDI and PDX are CEFs within the same PIMCO family, making their investment strategies and management styles similar. First, they both use leverage to increase returns, which also causes additional volatility in their NAV. Secondly, there's the active use of interest rate swaps to manage duration and hedge risks. Thirdly, there's a focus on generating monthly distributions to shareholders. Finally, they have access to instruments of varying complexity that are unavailable to ordinary investors. These complex portfolios often include assets that are unfamiliar to the broader market.

Although PDI and PDX share significant fundamental similarities, the two funds differ in size, since the former's portfolio is 7.5 times larger than the latter's ($7.51 billion vs. $1.01 billion). Despite this, PDI’s expense rate is higher (4.46% vs. 3.29%) due to its larger scale. Generally speaking, the management fee for PDI is a weak point compared to other PIMCO funds. Like PDX, a distinctive feature of PDI’s investment strategy is its focus on debt instruments across multiple sectors. Nevertheless, MBS mortgage securities, derivatives, and high-yield bonds constitute the main assets in the PDI portfolio. As stated in the fund’s prospectus, a minimum of 25% of assets is invested in private MBS, whereas up to 40% is allocated to securities issued by entities in emerging markets. Its portfolio is very broad, with 1,508 holdings, and the top 10 assets account for 28.96% of the total.

In contrast to PDI, a PDX portfolio strategy allows for a significant portion of capital to be invested in the energy sector, rendering it a multi-sector fund with a broader approach to investing in tactical opportunities. PDX's advantage over the PDI is its lower dependence on the mortgage market due to its higher exposure to the corporate sector and real assets. PDX's portfolio itself is less broad than PDI's, containing 340 holdings. Its top-10 asset concentration is higher at 50.41%, where a fairly large portion is invested in Venture Global (VG) (with an 11.39% weighting) and PIMCO Short Floating NAV Portfolio III Short-Term Instruments (with a 9.79% weighting).

Together with portfolio composition, another key difference between the funds is their use of leverage. With a current leverage ratio of 32.64%, PDI is considered an aggressive CEF. Therefore, in the event of a deep market correction, its NAV could decline more rapidly, representing a downside risk of investing in PDI during volatile market conditions. PDX’s credit strategy is slightly more conservative, using leverage at a level of 23.2%. Leverage costs amount to 4.09%, as opposed to PDI’s higher rate of 4.3%. As a result, they're exposed to a risk of forced asset sales in the event of a sharp market crash that's roughly one third lower.

The risks associated with changes in the P/NAV ratio also warrant closer examination. Historically, PDIs have traded at a premium of up to 20%. The average P/NAV ratio over the past five years had a premium of 9.4%. If we take the last five years, we see that the largest discount on PDI shares was 4.08%, whereas the premium was 19.76%. PDI shares are currently trading at a premium of 8.88%.

On the contrary, historically, shares of PDX have traded at a discount. The average P/NAV ratio over the past five years has been at a discount of 12.59%. If we take the last five years, PDX shares have traded at a maximum discount of 20.03%, with a premium of only 0.27%. The fund's shares are currently trading at a discount of 12.21%.

Also, between PDI and PDX, there are differences related to dividend policies, because PDI has an advantage in that the fund offers a higher yield (14.99% vs. 7.79%). Meanwhile, over the past five years, the fund has not made any changes to its payouts, whereas PDX's average annual dividend growth rate over five years is 11.96%. Another important point is that PDX made a special payment in January 2026, the amount of which was almost twice the dividends for the entire year.

Results of the PDI + PDX Combination

In my view, one of the most compelling reasons for combining PDI and PDX is the ability to reduce the weight of PDI in an income-focused portfolio, where half of the previously allocated capital would be redirected to PDX. The combination of PDI and PDX allows me to generate a more stable dividend income with less risk of a sharp decline in one of the funds, because there's a sectoral breakdown between PDI and PDX. This combination allows you to select a proven fund that serves as a PIMCO benchmark. In addition, I have added an asset that, although highly volatile because of its portfolio composition, looks undervalued based on its P/NAV ratio. These factors have the following effects:

  • Thanks to the purchase of PDX at a 12.21% discount, the previously paid PDI premium—which currently stands at 8.88%—is offset. This results in an overall P/NAV discount of 1.67%.
  • This creates sectoral diversification, with the PDI portfolio providing significant exposure to mortgage securities and structured credit, while PDX adds substantial weight to energy and corporate credit. This makes the portfolio less dependent on the real estate market (although it is important to note that during periods of panic among market participants, PIMCO's entire CEF family tends to fall in tandem as a result of forced deleveraging).
  • This smoothes out payment volatility, since PDI has a high yield of around 15%, whereas PDX’s yield is less than 8%. The combination of the two allows for maintaining a double-digit yield while offering the potential for special payments from PDX (on Jan. 13, 2026, for example, there was a payment of $2.97 per share).

A 50/50 portfolio allocation between PDI and PDX should produce the following results.

I use historical total return figures as one of the key indicators for asset valuation, allowing me to identify not only the trend in dividend payments but also how capital is preserved and grown. Analyzing the figures for the past year, the advantage is on the side of PDX (7.93%), whereas the total return for PDI was 3.79%.

For a three-year period, PDX performed similarly, generating a total return of 115.01%, compared to 50.30% for PDI. In this case, there's a more significant difference, proving the high attractiveness of the "younger brother" for a portfolio focused not only on generating high passive income but also on ensuring capital growth.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

In spite of the combined effect of PDI and PDX, there are a number of risks associated with this investment strategy, which are important to consider for each instrument. Firstly, actual results of PIMCO funds are influenced by their funding costs when raising capital through leverage. By 2026, we expect the yield curve to shift toward a compression of the spread between short-term funding costs and long-term bond yields. This trend, when sustained, would pose a risk of reduced fund dividends. Secondly, PDI's use of ROC poses a risk. As an example, out of $2.65 per share in 2024, ROC accounted for $1.26. ROC is a destructive practice, as the fund is unable to pay the stated dividend amount from NII. For more details on the risks associated with how changes in interest rates affect PDI’s payments, check out my previous article.

Finally, significant risks are associated with the quality of the assets in the funds’ portfolios. The PDI, for example, primarily invests in bonds with credit ratings of Below B (37.10% of the total portfolio) and B (27.62% of the total portfolio). Bonds with high credit ratings ranging from A to AAA account for a total of 19.05%. In the event of problems in the U.S. economy, defaults could increase, negatively impacting the quality of PDI’s portfolio and leading to a decline in its NAV.

Portfolio PDX also invests in bonds with low credit ratings. Bonds with Below B and B ratings account for 36.74% and 23.43% of the portfolio, respectively. In contrast, high credit rated bonds make up a smaller portion, at 16.27%. But PDX has an advantage in that it includes a portion of equity assets. Equity accounts for 26.62% of the fund’s total portfolio.

Conclusion

This investment strategy, which combines exposure to the PDI and PDX, helps reduce the risk associated with the PDI’s premium through the discount of the PDX. This also provides sectoral diversification as the PDX portfolio includes assets from the energy sector. The risk associated with investing in low-rated bonds managed by PIMCO remains, requiring special attention when monitoring changes in the yield curve. Should the US economy face more significant inflation-related challenges, these could lead to a shift in the Fed’s monetary policy. The result would be a reassessment of the ratings for those assets that are more sensitive to interest rates. Given that PDI and PDX employ high leverage, buying and holding them will remain a valid strategy if monetary policy continues to favor lowering interest rates or at least keeping them at current levels. For now, my recommendation for PDI remains "Buy," though I suggest allocating a portion of capital to invest in PDX.

and Rushabh Amin

Allspring Global Investments (UK) Limited

Category: Moderate allocation

Fund Strategy

  • Invests in affiliated mutual funds that collectively provide diversified exposure across and within a range of asset classes and subasset classes
  • Determines strategic weights of portfolio allocations based on portfolio management team's research that produces capital market assumptions used in combination with risk assessments and correlations
  • Employs a Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA) Overlay strategy that combines quantitative models and qualitative judgments and uses futures contracts and other instruments to implement portfolio tilts toward assets with higher expected returns or lower expected risk

Quarterly review

  • The fund (EAAIX) underperformed its custom benchmark, a blend of the Russell 3000 Index (45%), the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net) (20%), and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (35%), during the quarter.
  • Our tactical asset allocation positioning added to overall relative performance.
  • Challenging relative performance from value equity investments had the largest negative impact on relative performance.

Market review

Global equities maintained strong momentum, with emerging markets and developed non-U.S. leading the rally. The S&P 500 Index rose 2.66% for a 2025 gain of 17.88% compared with emerging markets, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net), up 33.57%. Optimism around information technology capital expenditures seemed to take a back seat, as energy and industrials outperformed the benchmark, with consumer discretionary the worst off. Fixed income markets remained disperse as we saw fixed income markets bounce back mainly in credit as opposed to sovereigns. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield started the quarter at 4.15% and closed at 4.17%, after trading as low as 3.95% in October, with the eurozone and Japan as relative underperformers, up 0.14% and 0.36%, respectively. Within credit, U.S. investment grade was up 0.84%, with high yield up 1.1% and emerging market debt up 2.4%. Precious metals stood out over the quarter. Gold closed the year at $4,319, up 65% (and 12% in the final quarter).

Amid the U.S. government's shutdown in the fourth quarter, macroeconomic data remained sparse, though underlying activity remained robust. U.S. growth continued to exceed expectations, despite a weaker labor market leading the Federal Reserve (Fed) to cut two times in the fourth quarter—further loosening policy. Japan has remained an anomaly to the global easing cycle; with a strong expected rebound in gross domestic product and inflation moving above the Bank of Japan's target, it saw them finally hike. The eurozone continued resilience with moderate growth, supported by domestic demand, a strong labor market, and easing inflation. Emerging market growth was strong over the quarter, particularly in Latin America.

Portfolio positioning

OVERALL ASSET ALLOCATION

  • The portfolio's neutral allocation target remains 35% fixed income, 45% U.S. equity, and 20% foreign equity. At quarter-end, the tactical positioning reflected an underweight to both U.S. and non-U.S. equities, a curve-steepening bias in U.S. fixed income, and a positive view on the U.S. dollar. Long-equity exposure was expressed through outright long positions in the Nasdaq, the S&P 500 Index, Japan, and emerging markets. Fixed income views were implemented via U.S. curve steepeners. UNDERLYING FUNDS

Portfolio positioning

OUTLOOK

  • In the U.S., fiscal stimulus may peak in early 2026, with gradual monetary easing expected absent significant labor market deterioration. Continued weakness—whether from immigration restrictions, federal layoffs, or labor-saving artificial intelligence (AI) adoption—could prompt additional Fed cuts if tariff-driven inflation proves short-lived.
  • In the eurozone, fiscal support is likely to take longer to flow through the economy than markets anticipate, raising the risk of inflation undershooting. Japan is progressing toward a higher-rate regime, supported by firm inflation, strong growth, and potential fiscal easing. Other economies could also see rate hikes as policy becomes less restrictive and recession risks recede. China continues to employ fiscal measures aimed at stabilizing sentiment.
  • U.S. growth remains supported by policy, with 2025's AI-driven productivity gains offering a buffer. Japan should also stay resilient, while eurozone expectations appear toward the upper bound of what is achievable. Emerging markets continue to demonstrate growth resilience.
  • As inflation moves closer to central bank targets, risks remain—particularly from tariff pass-throughs.
