data.title: Santos: Practically A Bullish Natural Gas Pure Play
data.description:
This article is about why I'm bullish about Australia's Santos Limited (STOSF).

Regular readers of my articles will know that I'm structurally bullish on the natural gas sector. That's because the world needs clean reliable baseload energy and electricity demand is growing thanks to China's rapid adoption of electric vehicles and data centers' vast appetite for electricity.

The energy crisis in the Middle East is a new variable to consider as well. As I write up this article, I see two disturbing news headlines, first 17% of Qatar's LNG capacity has been knocked out, and second, according to QatarEnergy, this could take five years to repair. The world is going to need US and Australian LNG more than ever now.

Trade publication Upstream quoted QatarEnergy chief executive Saad Sherida Al-Kaabi “The impact is on China, South Korea, Italy and Belgium. This means that we will be compelled to declare force majeure for up to five years on some long-term LNG contracts.”

For China and South Korea, the obvious replacement gas is from Australia.

Recently, I upgraded Woodside Energy (WDS), Australia's largest energy company. Its second largest energy company is Santos, which I last covered at the end of 2021, following its acquisition of Oil Search for A$21bn which I noted created a powerhouse in LNG production.

I'm going to update the thesis on Santos here.

Assets - oil and gas proved reserves

First, let's get back to basics. The core value of a commodity producer is its proved reserves of commodities. Since both Woodside and Santos have recently published their 2025 annual reports, we can compare their reserves.

At 31 December 2025, Woodside had 1,882.1 million barrels of oil equivalent of total proved reserves. This includes 7,637.1 billion cubic feet of natural gas, or converting at 5.7 conversion factor, 1.3 billion barrels of oil equivalent, or 71% of the total.

At 31 December 2025, Santos had 913 million barrels of oil equivalent of total proved reserves. This includes 4,494 petajoules, PJ, of sales gas, or converting at one PJ equals 171,937 barrels of oil equivalent, 0.8 billion barrels of oil equivalent, or 85% of the total.

In short, while both Woodside and Santos are predominately natural gas companies, Santos is even more "gassy" based on its proved reserves.

This will explain why the Santos stock price has been rallying in recent days following the force majeures from QatarEnergy.

A quick detour

In June 2025 Santos received a takeover offer from a consortium led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Company's foreign investment arm XRG worth some $18.72 billion. By September that deal had collapsed. According to the Australian Financial Review, XRG said it had "found factors during its due diligence work on Santos that had reduced its assessment of the target’s value." The offer had been for 8.89 Australian dollars a share. At the time of writing Santos trades in Australia for 7.98 Australian dollars a share. Interestingly the board of XRG includes Jon Gray, president and COO of Blackstone, and Bernard Looney, the former CEO of BP plc.

We will never be exactly privy to what XRG's due diligence came up with but the Wall Street Journal reported "XRG said the decision not to proceed followed a comprehensive evaluation of all commercial factors and terms included in the agreement required by the Santos board." This hints of boardroom issues rather than asset quality. But XRG's loss is our gain.

Santos' portfolio

Santos has a regional operating model, with the three regional business units being Eastern Australia and Papua New Guinea, Western and Northern Australia and Timor-Leste, and Alaska. According to the Santos annual report "The Alaska Business Unit is currently captured in the ‘Corporate, exploration, eliminations & other’ segment information in the Financial Report while the asset is in the development phase."

First I will list each region by 2025 "Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and depletion, exploration and evaluation expensed, net impairment loss/reversal and change in future restoration assumptions" or EBITDAX, in US dollars.

Midstream and Energy Solutions: $92m

Eastern Australia and PNG

Cooper Basin Upstream: $208m

Queensland and New South Wales: $634m

Papua New Guinea: $2,046m

Western Australia, Northern Australia, Timor-Leste

Northern Australia and Timor-Leste: loss $37m

Western Australia: $540m

With Corporate, exploration, eliminations, & other having an EBITDAX of negative $92m, total EBITDAX for Santos in 2025 was $3,391 million. We can see immediately that Papua New Guinea accounts for 60% and is the crown jewel for Santos. Similar to proved reserves, we see that "Gas, ethane and liquefied natural gas" dominated total product sales, accounting for $4,174m from total product sales in 2025 of $4,939 million, or 85%.

Though EBITDAX was down 9% on 2024, production volume was up 1% in 2025.

Liquefied Natural Gas

Santos’ portfolio includes three world-class LNG projects: PNG LNG, Gladstone LNG and Darwin LNG. These projects are an important supplier of LNG to the Asia–Pacific region, and each project is co-owned by Japanese and/or Korean partners.

Santos’ interest in the PNG LNG project is 39.9%. Exxon Mobil (XOM) is the operator and owns a 33.2% stake. Japan's ENEOS Holdings owns 4.7%, and the remaining interests are owned by Papua New Guinea. PNG LNG shipped 116 cargoes in 2025.

Santos has a 30% interest in Gladstone LNG and operates the upstream fields (Fairview, Roma, Arcadia and Scotia), which provide feed gas to the GLNG plant. The other participants in the project are Petronas, TotalEnergies (TTE), and Korea Gas Corporation. In 2025, GLNG shipped 101 cargoes.

Santos has an interest in Darwin LNG of 43.4% and operates the liquefaction facility. The other shareholders are Korea's SK, Japan's INPEX, JERA, and Tokyo Gas, and Italy's Eni (E). Barossa LNG is providing a new source of gas to the existing Darwin LNG plant, and Barossa gas is now extending Darwin LNG's operating life for around 20 years. The original gas field supplying Darwin LNG, Bayu-Undan, is now being converted into a carbon capture and storage project.

Risks

Clearly, as with any commodity producer, market price risk is a key consideration. Interestingly, in its 2025 annual report, Santos notes that in order to mitigate some price risk, "A number of mid and long-term LNG sales have also been executed including with QatarEnergy Trading, TotalEnergies, Glencore, Hokkaido Gas and Shizuoka Gas."

It's good to see that Santos has a relationship with QatarEnergy, as Qatar is likely going to want to purchase third party LNG in order to meet some of the obligations it wouldn't otherwise be able to meet thanks to the damage caused by the Iran war.

Santos' annual report also states that "All oil, a majority of the LNG and a portion of the gas produced in our portfolio are sold under sales contracts where the sale price is linked to global benchmark prices for oil, such as Brent crude. Spot sales of our LNG are predominantly sold at prices linked to either global benchmark prices for oil or the Platts Japan-Korea-Marker (JKM), which is the LNG benchmark price assessment for spot physical cargoes."

While Santos does have an active marketing team, and this is why sales volumes sometimes differ from production volumes, it's not clear what the split of LNG sales is between contracts and spot sales, and hence where the commodity price risk really lies, more with crude oil or more with LNG benchmarks?

Growth Capex

The most interesting growth project for Santos, in my opinion, is the Papua LNG project (Santos 22.8% interest), which is a proposed LNG project that would "share certain midstream infrastructure with PNG LNG" according to the Santos annual report. TotalEnergies is the operator of this project, and engineering work "continues to advance all workstreams in support of progress toward final investment decision" and achieving final investment decision is a strategic priority for Santos for 2026.

Valuation

Santos trades on a trailing P/E of 21x. This is higher than Woodside's 16x, but in line with other large exploration and production companies such as ConocoPhillips (COP) at 20x, or gas focused EQT Corp (EQT) at 20x. Santos could see some multiple expansion since Santos is well placed to supply Asian buyers with lost Qatari gas and the company is differentiated in that respect as one of the few LNG suppliers in the region. In addition, Santos has committed to return at least 60% of free cash flow to shareholders, with the remaining 40% to reduce debt or increase shareholder returns.

Conclusion

In the short term, Santos is bound to become a hot stock thanks to the Middle East and QatarEnergy crisis. Longer term, with the world needing more gas, and Santos being a leading gas supplier to Asia with big LNG projects in Australia and Papua New Guinea, I expect Santos to be able to grow revenues and earnings. It would be nice if Santos could disclose like Woodside its mix of commodity pricing - Woodside is moving more to portfolio LNG sales linked to spot LNG market pricing, but with the Oil Search acquisition well and truly integrated, Santos can focus on paying down its debt and rebuilding its Darwin LNG production, knowing it has the right assets at the right time in the right location. In other words, Santos has strong tailwinds and may itself be the target for another takeover bid given its attractive asset base and opportunity set, in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Are Souring On Trump's Iran Strategy
data.description:
Thesis Summary

We’re now into week three of the Iran war, and there still seems to be no end in sight.

What should have been a swift, surgical joint U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran has turned into something much bigger.

Iran is not Venezuela, with its 90 million inhabitants and strategic position and assets in the Middle East.

Today, the S&P 5000 finally broke below its 200 EMA, and as if Trump were tracking this, he quickly made two key announcements to lift stocks back up.

But the damage may be done, and even though Trump is trying to backtrack, Iran may not let him.

Let’s break down the latest in the Iran war below,

A War Built on Mixed Signals

Trump told CBS News on Monday that the war was "very complete, pretty much," claiming Iran's military capabilities had been wiped out.

But in my opinion, and I think one shared by markets, this doesn’t line up with much of the other news we’ve heard over the last week, which, if anything, points towards escalation.

We're now three weeks into this war, and Iran doesn't seem to want out. Tehran has retaliated with increased attacks on Israeli and U.S. assets across the Gulf region.

It’s no longer just oil ships now being able to move, but actual production facilities, like Qatar’s LNG, which are being hit.

If the war’s almost over, as Trump says, no one has told Iran.

Iran's foreign minister told PBS NewsHour that negotiating with the United States would not "be on the table."

Then Trump Backed Down

During these last three weeks, the market could be described as volatile but definitely in a downtrend.

Down over 6% since the all-time highs, the S&P 500 actually fell below its 200-week EMA today.

But as I write, the indexes are up 1%. This could be a technical bounce, but it’s likely driven by some key developments.

Recent news suggests that the U.S. may actually lift sanctions on Iranian oil. This could help alleviate global demand, but it seems like a Band-Aid on a much larger problem.

This Is Not Bullish

While markets are cheering for now, this is not something that seems bullish in the long term.

It’s a clear show that the U.S. has bitten off more than it can chew.

The operation is dragging on longer than expected, and it seems like a recession is a statistical likelihood now.

In all instances where oil has risen to these levels, a recession has followed.

And while the U.S. struggles to contain the situation in the Middle East, the real issue continues to escalate.

Meanwhile, China Circles…

As I’ve written about before, this is all an indirect attack on China. Both Iran and Venezuela were allies and energy suppliers to the country.

As a response, the situation with Taiwan is heating up, and the U.S. has even come out and stated it does not see China invading Taiwan as likely in 2027.

The fact that you have to come out and say this, to me, is not a very bullish sign.

Markets Don’t Trust Trump

The larger issue at hand here is that Trump has lost a lot of credibility in the last few weeks. His portrayal of the war has been rightfully met with skepticism.

And on many other fronts, he is also alienating the U.S. population.

Historically, this doesn’t bode well for markets.

Yes, in the long term, the market balances out and tends to shrug off political turmoil, but there have been so many instances where we can directly link market turmoil to the actions of the government.

The Great Depression followed the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, which devastated global commerce and popped the 1920s stock market bubble.

In 1971, we had the Nixon shock, which led to the closing of the gold window and at least contributed towards the following decade of stagflation.

And in 1979, Jimmy Carter had the "Crisis of Confidence”.

An energy crisis ended a period of growth and led to an increase in both inflation and interest rates as the economy struggled.

Eventually, Carter fired his entire cabinet, which fueled the perception of an erratic presidency without direction.

Could something similar happen today? It certainly doesn’t seem implausible.

What Should Investors Do?

The current macroeconomic climate is not favorable to stocks.

The Fed came out rather hawkish. We have an ongoing war and the added volatility of this being a midterm year.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has not moved in six months, and over the last month, every rally has been sold

This is not the time to take risks.

Discipline is important, and while I do think we will eventually see a return to growth, especially as AI productivity kicks in, the next 2-3 months could see markets retest important support levels.

Final Thoughts

Markets listen to Trump, and headlines matter. For a while, the President had good control over the narrative, but this is beginning to change.

The market is losing trust, and this creates uncertainty over the future, which is not good for returns.

The next few months will be challenging and, arguably, a make-or-break point that will define what the rest of this presidency looks like politically and economically.
